data.title: Two Harbors: The Merger That Might Not Be
data.description:
Thesis

Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) capital structure owners were ecstatic back in December 2025 when the merger between TWO and UWM Holdings Corporation (UWMC) was announced. The common equity jumped on the day, while the preferred shares and bonds rallied in the subsequent weeks. Things however are turning differently than expected, and in this article we are going to present readers the market signals that indicate the merger might not happen unless terms change. Furthermore we are going to highlight the impact of the binary events on the Series A Preferred Shares (TWO.PR.A), securities which we hold as well.

Initial merger terms

UWMC is a much larger corporation, with a $5.8 billion market value, and a much better credit via its unsecured funding rates. The proposed merger was a fairly standard but conservative one, with only stock for stock consideration:

PONTIAC, Mich. & NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--UWM Holdings Corporation (“UWMC”) and Two Harbors Investment Corp. (“TWO”) (NYSE: TWO), today announced that they have entered into a definitive merger agreement pursuant to which UWMC will acquire TWO in an all-stock transaction for $1.3 billion in equity value, based on a fixed exchange ratio of 2.3328x. The transaction has been unanimously approved by the Boards of Directors of both UWMC and TWO. The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, subject to approval of TWO’s stockholders and the satisfaction of other closing conditions, including customary regulatory approvals.

Now, as we can see from the above, TWO holders would get a fixed amount of UWMC shares upon the merger completion, thus be fully subject to the equity fluctuations of the UWMC stock. Some merger transactions contain a fixed payment provision to further incentivize holders to vote for the event. Not here.

Basically the selling point for the TWO common shareholders is that their equity stake is set to appreciate in value as part of a larger, more diverse company as UWMC.

As per the announcement, while the boards of directors approved the merger, it was still subject to common shareholders vote. If the TWO common shareholders do not vote in favor, the merger does not happen.

Market signs of trouble

The way things work in the markets is that investors try to anticipate what will happen and vote via the instruments available to them. On the fixed income side, we saw a massive rally in the TWO preferred equity, since UWMC expressed a view that they would retire it upon the merger completion due to the high cost of capital:

TWO.PR.A rallied over 6%, while TWO.PR.C virtually touched par given its first call date in 2026. Now the fixed income securities basically were baking in a successful merger and an upgrade in terms of both credit risk as well as call risk. Quite the rally indeed.

On the common equity side, after such an announcement, the two equities need to trade in tandem if the market believes in the merger. And in the beginning they did:

Notice, though, that since the beginning of March, things have changed. There were slight daily differences, followed by massive divergences in the past days. Why? Because the TWO shareholders seem to have a diverging view on the merger now.

Postponement of merger vote

On March 16, 2026 the vote was supposed to happen for the TWO shareholders:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) adjourned its special meeting of stockholders on Monday to provide additional time for its shareholders to vote on the proposed acquisition of the mortgage servicing rights-focused REIT by UWM Holdings (UWMC), the company said.

To translate the above in English: We do not have cvorum, so we will postpone the meeting. Because it appears the management team realized they would not get a 'Yes' vote, they postponed the meeting. UWMC is also trying to showcase how fantastic of a company it is, with an impromptu business update:

The company is trying to convince TWO holders that its upside is very large and they should vote in favor of the corporate action. In the chart above they highlight market consensus for their common equity and the upside for TWO holders if they vote in favor of the corporate action.

Furthermore, UWMC management even engaged an external party to help with the strategy around the merger:

It is clear as day that UWMC wants this merger to occur, but the stock price for the company does not help. If the UWMC common shares would have rallied this year, the merger likely would have already been approved. It is not the case, though. The main issue is that the TWO book value is at $11.13 per share as of the latest reporting, while the implied value from the UWMC shares is significantly below that. Why approve a merger where you get shares for a value below book? That is the crux of the issue.

Our view on the forward

Unless we see a market rally from here or an improvement in the UWMC offer, we do not see the merger going through. The market has already voted and said that in the current format the math does not work out. The macro is complicated by the war in Iran, higher rates and inflation fears, all which are pushing rates higher and the UWMC stock price lower.

Impact to TWO.PR.A

We are holders of TWO.PR.A, and we are very sad regarding the current events since the move here is lower. The securities rallied by virtue of being upgraded by the market. UWMC is a much better credit, and the market started pricing TWO.PR.A as such as well. If the merger completely falls through we are going to see a return to higher yields and lower prices:

Before the merger announcement TWO.PR.A was trading with a 23-handle. We are afraid to say it, but we might be heading right back there unless there is an improvement in the merger offer or the market rallies hard.

Latest developments

As we were putting the finishing touches on our article, the TWO management communicated that there is a competing offer to the UWMC one:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) stock surged 11% in Thursday premarket trading after it received an unsolicited proposal for $10.70 per share in cash, the company said on Thursday. That comes after the mortgage REIT had already agreed to be acquired by mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC) in an all-stock deal that valued each share of TWO at $11.94 on Dec. 16, 2025. At the close of March 18, the UWMC deal valued each share of TWO at ~$8.61.

The common shares rallied hard, while the preferred shares slumped. The reasoning is that there are likely no other highly rated names like UWMC who would take over; thus, while the commons are favored by the cash offer, the preferred shares are not. As a Series A preferred holder, we would like to see the best rated counterpart take over, so that credit spreads tighten. We do not care that much about how the common holders get compensated.

We have to admit, though, that at this juncture, unless UWMC revises their offer, the balance is tilted towards TWO going with the competing offer, which is not advantageous for the preferred holders. All is not lost yet, though, and we are keenly waiting to see if UWMC can send a revised bid given the effort they have put so far into the merger.

Conclusion

TWO.PR.A are preferred shares from Two Harbors (TWO). The securities rallied hard upon the news of the UWMC merger, but have given back some of the gains as the market is beginning to doubt the corporate event. Unless there are changes in the offer or the overall market rallies hard, we do not see the merger going through. This would mean a return to a $23 level price for TWO.PR.A, while an approved merger would see the securities rally back by 4% and have a better volatility profile going forward. We are holding and hoping for an offer improvement by UWMC.

data.title: AT&T: Way Too Late To Be Buying Now
data.description:
AT&T: Keep Calm and Carry On

I don't think AT&T (T) investors have had much to complain about recently, as the company’s fourth-quarter earnings release in late January and the guidance indicate an overly aggressive capital expenditure outlay is clearly a thing of the past, even though those numbers are still expected to stay at a high level. Nevertheless, the telco operator is still delivering pretty solid free cash flow numbers, and ones that suggest the medium-term outlook should not see the company sacrificing margins in favor of pursuing an ill-disciplined capital allocation regime, which should help to alleviate the fears of income investors who could be concerned about the debt leverage encompassing such a strategy. Back in my previous AT&T write-up in October 2025, I made a noteworthy mention about the company's more assured capital spending and relatively stable free cash flow outlook. The market appears to be sending the right signals about T so far, as the stock has remained incredibly resilient.

However, big numbers were thrown about recently, as AT&T is reportedly looking to “spend $250B building out its network over the next five years.” That caught my eye because it was only in January when T provided what I thought to be a reasonably comfortable range in terms of capital expenditure for investors to digest and take away from, since those are annualized figures to start with. In essence, management promulgated an annual CapEx outlay of $23.5B at the midpoint while also enunciating steadily increasing free cash flow numbers of $18B+ for this year before reaching more than 21 billion dollars by FY2028.

So, why is AT&T looking to shake the boat so soon after posting its full-year guidance just less than two months ago? Fortuitously, those figures included long-term operating costs, not just CapEx. Importantly, no changes were made to the outlook for CapEx guidance, which would have necessitated significant changes to analyst estimates for the next five years. Income investors who have invested consistently with AT&T over the past few years shouldn't be alarmed by the headline figure, as the key CapEx metrics are, after all, not materially impacted.

AT&T's Broadband Satellite Forays Offers Optionality but Not Near-Term Drivers

Nevertheless, I could see that AT&T is leaning in further with the AST SpaceMobile collaboration (ASTS) to “extend coverage into remote areas,” and making broadband satellite connectivity one of its major growth pillars through the rest of the decade. I thought the timing was especially apt, since the much-anticipated SpaceX’s IPO (SPACE) could make such offerings more mainstream while allowing the Elon Musk-led company a lot more leverage and financial capabilities to proliferate its leadership and expand its market exposure into new growth areas while competing with AT&T. These are exciting milestones that could elevate investment sentiments, right? But in my opinion, I think it's still too nascent for us to ascertain the success of the partnership. After all, the company’s current strategy is still based on its convergence framework. And that’s largely predicated upon the success of offering “historically separate networks like 5G, fiber optic broadband, and Wi-Fi on one platform.”

Therefore, I believe the primary gameplay for AT&T in the near term will still focus on this framework while building out its broadband satellite connectivity business to be a more significant contributor subsequently. It’s notable that “42% of households that have AT&T fiber now also choose AT&T for wireless,” giving the company a clear runway to increase its penetration rate over time. And, AT&T has also emphasized the importance of this thesis during a recent conference, as they explained:

Just to level-set, we ended last year with 32 million fiber passings. With the closing of the Lumen transaction, we are now over 36 million. We expect to end this year at 40 million fiber passings. So incrementally, for this year, we are adding over 8 million fiber locations. With each of those fiber locations comes a convergence opportunity. You should expect us to drive incremental share take in -- where we are expanding our footprint, plus the build that -- the existing build, which there's still opportunity to drive further penetration. And that's going to drive not only fiber volumes, but that will drive also wireless. I mentioned earlier, our penetration in the Lumen footprint, of convergence, the convergence in the Lumen footprint is significantly less than our own. And so you should expect us to really focus on driving incremental convergence in that footprint. So those 2 things should allow us to really drive share take in fiber and mobility. - AT&T Inc. Presents at Deutsche Bank 34th Annual Media, Internet & Telecom Conference Transcript

I thought these were crucial guideposts for investors to contemplate because they give us confidence that the convergence narrative is working fine, even as the company seeks a new, more exciting growth vector outside its current core capabilities.

Given that AT&T’s forward earnings multiple of 11.9x is not quite configured for growth (where's the implied premium?), I do not believe it will feature prominently among the shareholder class demanding clear visibility into the satellite connectivity business for now. That will definitely remain a work in progress with future optionality, and I believe the market will consider it just fine, as AT&T focuses on its core business while working to return to its target leverage ratio of 2.5x and delivering on its promises of hitting >$21B of FCF by 2028, less than two FYs from now.

AT&T Must Maintain Capital Allocation Discipline

Wall Street is largely confident that AT&T can deliver on its outlook, slotting in the FCF outlook for 2028 at just under $21B. Given the visibility and relatively solid metrics that T has posted recently, while also having a less onerous regulatory climate, I feel confident that the company should be able to sustain its incremental share gains through its recent integration with the Lumen transaction, expanding its footprint.

Notably, we could expect some near-term pressure on expecting a materially higher ARPU as part of the convergence story, with imputed discounts to encourage signups, but I don't expect the overall margins to be markedly affected. While the opportunity for longer-term accretion could be a welcome growth lever, I think the cautious optimism indicated by management should help to provide some flexibility for the company to grow into its expanded opportunity with the Lumen deal, as they emphasized that they “wouldn't expect a meaningful contribution from ARPU for both wireless and fiber.”

Buy Or Sell T Stock?

So after we have sorted out its fundamentals, its near-term growth drivers, and also the optionality emanating from its partnership with AST SpaceMobile, I believe the optimism has already been somewhat captured in T’s price action. As we can glean from the above chart, T buyers have been unable to muster a decisive breakthrough above the $30 zone, making it a significant resistance level that could deny further upside unless we see a more profound upgrade to its medium-term outlook. Failing which, I believe the stock should continue to hover around or below these levels, even though the bearish downtrend is well and truly over.

T’s relatively cheap multiples should still help keep income investors on board, although the forward yield of 4% is arguably too low if I were to consider that the 10Y is still printing at 4.3%, which offers a much more competitive rate.

Hence, I think the market is somewhat quite nonchalant about AT&T's buying opportunity at the current level, but I could see a potential entry point for opportunistic investors to consider. If a further fallout in the equity markets were to allow the stock to decline back to just above the $20 level, then I think another opportunity to get more engaged might just materialize. Until then, I think we should just allow this market action to play out and remain on the sidelines for now.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: How Much Does Management Matter For A Publicly Traded Company?
data.description:
When we reviewed the carnage among business development companies, or BDCs, when recapping February 2026's dividend decreases, its concentration within this sub-sector of the financial services sector of the U.S. economy really stood out.

BDCs make their money by loaning money they either raise from investors or borrow themselves to small- and medium-sized enterprises that can't raise money by going public and selling stock and also financially distressed businesses. The business models of most established BDCs involve borrowing money, then loaning it back out at higher interest rates, where they pocket the difference.

That makes the profit margins of BDCs vulnerable to rate cuts. Because their loans are tied to the Federal Funds Rate, when the Fed cuts that rate, it negatively impacts BDC profits. In the last three years, BDCs have gone from a rising or high interest rate environment (March 2023 through August 2025) to a falling rate environment (September 2024 through December 2025).

The performance of the VanEck BDC Income Exchange Traded Fund (BIZD), which includes over 30 BDCs in its market cap-weighted index, gives a good sense of how BDCs performed in these different environments. The following chart shows BDCs rising or flat in the rising rate environment, but then either stalling or falling as the Fed shifted gears into its rate-cutting mode.

But that's not the whole story. During the rate-cutting period, which initiated the pressure on BDC profits, BDCs have had to cope with the DeepSeek AI shock, peaking just ahead of that event on 19 February 2025. Then they faced the Liberation Day global tariffs shock event of 2 April 2025, plunging with the rest of the market before going on to recover. That lasted until August 2025, when the return of rate cuts initiated a new downtrend that was followed in January 2026 with a new AI shock event that undermined the business prospects of the Software-As-A-Service (SaaS) firms. Many of which were getting their funding to grow from BDCs.

With AI technologies seemingly set to destroy any potential profitability these firms had, many BDCs were suddenly faced with having to write down large portions of their portfolios. But not all BDCs are in that boat.

When we looked at the stock performance of individual BDCs, we found a clear characteristic that divided them. That characteristic is their governance, and what quickly became evident was that internally managed BDCs were generally outperforming BDCs whose investments are managed by external parties.

To illustrate that difference, we randomly selected six externally managed BDCs to compare their performance against an equal number of internally managed BDCs over the last three years. Here is a list of the BDCs in our performance sample:

Externally Managed BDCs

  • Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC)
  • Fidus Investment (FDUS)
  • Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC)
  • Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL)
  • Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending (NCDL)
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX)

Internally Managed BDCs

  • Capital Southwest (CSWC)
  • Gladstone Capital (GLAD)
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN)
  • Phenixfin (PFX)
  • Rand Capital (RAND)
  • Trinity Capital (TRIN)

Let's get to the results. The following chart visualizes the relative performance of the stocks of the two kinds of BDCs:

We've shown the 3-year returns for the benchmarks of the S&P 500 (SPX) at 72.01% and BIZD at -10.79% to show how they compare against the range of the two categories. The externally managed BDCs range from a high of +2.79% to a low of -29.22%, with four of the six BDCs having a negative return.

By contrast, the internally managed BDCs range from a high of +44.22% to a low of -16.96%, with two of the six BDCs having a negative return.

But it's not just recent market events driving that outcome. In the next two charts, we show how the sample of internally managed and externally managed BDCs compares with the performance of the S&P 500 over the last three years. The first chart tracks the internally managed BDCs:

The next chart follows the externally managed BDCs over the same period.

We find the internally managed BDCs have sustained better performance than the externally managed BDCs over all portions of this three-year period, which can be seen in their relative performance being closer to that of the benchmark S&P 500 index. That better performance occurred both in a period in which rising interest rates provided BDCs with a tailwind and the current period in which falling interest rates are providing fierce headwinds against the BDCs.

When we started this exercise, we thought we'd mainly be discussing the role of how changing interest rates have affected the performance of the BDC sub-sector of the financial services industry, leading so many of these firms to cut their dividends in recent months. We didn't expect to run into a more interesting question: how much does management matter in a publicly traded company? In the case of BDCs, whether the people managing their lending business work directly for the firm or are employed outside of it would appear to have a significant impact affecting the returns of the shareholders who own the companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Best Stocks With Forward EPS Growth Above 40%
data.description:
Strong Earnings Outlook Bolsters Market

The Fed’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged, hotter-than-expected inflation data, and surging oil prices triggered a broad market selloff that spared no sector. However, prior to this pullback, markets had weathered volatility tied to the Iran war and concerns over stretched valuations.

The key has been robust earnings forecasts, which have improved since the outbreak of hostilities. S&P 500 earnings are expected to grow by 15.3% in CY 2026 and 11.6% in Q1, which would mark a sixth-straight quarter of double-digit YoY growth. Q1 growth estimates are being led by AI-fueled technology sector stocks (+42% YoY), followed by materials and financials, according to FactSet data.

Recent market volatility has pulled down a broad range of stocks, including companies with attractive fundamentals and solid earnings growth outlooks. In several cases, the decline appears driven more by external factors – such as shifting monetary policy expectations, geopolitical developments, and macroeconomic data – than by any deterioration in underlying business performance.

The opportunity lies in identifying high-quality companies where fundamentals remain intact, but valuations have compressed alongside broader market volatility. In this environment, a disciplined, data-driven approach can help isolate stocks with strong earnings growth, profitability, and valuation support.

How I Chose Top Stocks With Strong Forward EPS Growth

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I filtered for Strong Buys with high forward EPS growth rates and strong Growth and EPS Revision factors grades. I then narrowed the list to stocks that experienced recent pullbacks, including double-digit declines in the past 30 days that also possessed attractive valuations based on PEG ratios. Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each stock relative to sector peers and grades them across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Earnings Revisions, and Momentum.

1. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $18.76B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductor

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 18 out of 525

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 4 out of 69

Recently added to the PRO Quant Portfolio and a member of Alpha Picks, Credo is a developer of connectivity solutions for AI infrastructure and among the fastest growing microchip stocks in the past year, outpacing large-cap peers in earnings and revenue growth.

Shares fell this week when Nvidia (NVDA) said it would use copper and optical cables in parallel as it scales up next-generation AI systems, even though Credo supplies connectivity used in both technologies. In fact, BNP Paribas analyst Karl Ackerman sees Nvidia's dual-pronged approach as "bullish" for the company.

CRDO vs. S&P Semiconductor Select Index (XSD): 1Y Price Return

Despite another earnings beat and guidance lift, Credo’s stock pulled back after falling just short of high gross margin expectations. Credo’s revenue grew by over 200% to $407M in Q3 2026 and EPS of $1.07 beat by $0.13, driven by continued strength in active electrical cables (AECs) and integrated circuits (ICs).

Looking ahead, Credo expects new acquisitions and product upgrades to expand its addressable market across the full spectrum of data center connectivity – from optical networking to the chip level – to deliver energy-efficient AI clusters, according to CEO Bill Brennan.

“With continued growth in AECs and ICs and the announcement of three new multi-billion dollar TAM expansions through ZeroFlap optics, ALCs, and OmniConnect, we remain confident in our ability to innovate and grow in the expanding AI infrastructure landscape,” Brennan said in a press release.

With 14 upward earnings revisions to no down revisions in the past 90 days, analysts are increasingly bullish on Credo’s outlook. EPS is projected to grow by 372% in FY 2026 and revenue by over 200% to $1.33B. Expanding margins have been key drivers of Credo’s exceptional Profitability Grade, along with robust return on equity and capital.

Showcasing EPS forward growth of +275%, Credo’s soaring earnings prospects have lifted the stock’s valuation framework. A forward long-term EPS growth rate (3-5Y CAGR) of +78% also underpins an A+ Growth Grade, in addition to surging revenue and operating profit.

After the recent dip, the stock is now trading at a 69% discount to the sector based on its 0.39 PEG ratio - a valuation metric that combines P/E and long-term forward growth. The recent pullback offers investors an attractive opportunity, with Credo positioned to potentially expand market share and deliver on long-term growth expectations.

2. Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.63B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 49 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 7 out of 37

A Sunbelt road builder headquartered in Alabama, Construction Partners has benefited from reshoring trends, migration patterns, and the continued build-out of physical infrastructure fueled by the AI data center boom. Volatility fueled by geopolitical tensions and rate cut uncertainty saw the stock drop in recent weeks, slowing solid momentum – including a post-earnings surge in February.

ROAD vs. S&P 500 Industrial Sector (XLI): 1Y Price Return

Favorable weather and strength in commercial and public project demand helped drive strong Q1 2026 results, with revenue up 44% and adjusted EBITDA by +63%. ROAD closed the quarter with a record backlog of $3.09B, underscoring robust demand across all of the firm’s markets. The company is participating in 1,000 commercial sector projects across eight states and 110 local markets, and has seen an uptick in state and federal contracts as governments increase infrastructure investment to support growing Sunbelt economies.

Management said the company is focused on expanding its southern footprint through targeted M&A and organic initiatives with a goal of doubling revenue to $6B by 2030. ROAD’s growth estimates continue to climb, backed by solid earnings revision momentum, with EPS expected to rise by 32% in FY 2026 and revenue by 25% to $3.52B. Exceptional forward earnings growth and surging operating profit, cash flow, and return on equity have driven a top-notch Growth Grade.

Although trading at a premium to earnings, ROAD’s PEG is at a 36% discount to the sector. ROAD’s recent dip does not reflect the company’s strong underlying fundamentals. In addition, bullish earnings revisions and surging forward growth estimates make ROAD a top buy-the-dip stock.

3. China Yuchai International Limited (CYD)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.50B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction Machinery & Heavy Transportation Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 23 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/19/2026): 2 out of 28

Another PQP stock and leading powertrain solutions provider, CYD was caught in the broader selloff after the Fed decided to hold interest rates, but is still showcasing excellent momentum in the past three months. CYD has a strong foothold in the Chinese market but is boosting penetration in ASEAN while launching high-horsepower products to meet explosive data center-driven demand.

CYD significantly outpaced the market in truck and bus engine sales in FY 2025, driving revenue up by 28% to $3.5B, while sales to data centers rose to 2,000 units vs. 750 in the prior year. Expanding its footprint and strengthening market access across ASEAN, Europe, and the Americas has helped support a strong earnings outlook. The annual results contributed to an A+ Growth Grade, driven by strong forward EPS growth rates and surging cash flow, operating profit, and return on equity.

CYD is now trading at 14x earnings vs. the sector’s 19x, while PEG is at an 80% discount for an ‘A’ Valuation Grade. CYD also has very attractive EV/EBITDA, price/sales, and price/book multiples. CYD fell amid rate cut uncertainty and Middle East tensions, but offers investors an opportune entry point. CYD wraps up my top buy the dip picks with strong forward EPS growth – three stocks with solid fundamentals that are well-positioned for a rebound.

Other stocks with Strong Buy ratings and high earnings growth targets that have been beaten down recently include: Barrick Mining Corporation (B), Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE), Eldorado Gold Corporation (EGO), Gold Fields Limited (GFI), Ironwood Pharmaceuticals, Inc., (IRWD), Newmont Corporation (NEM), and Orla Mining Ltd. (ORLA).

Buy The Dip: Best Stocks With Strong Forward Earnings Growth

The war in Iran, interest rate cut uncertainty, and stubborn inflation sent the S&P 500 on a roller coaster ride this week, although, on the year, the market has held steady due in no small part to strong earnings outlooks. S&P 500 earnings are projected to rise by double digits in Q1 2026, led by big tech stocks riding AI tailwinds. The recent selloff has hit a wide range of stocks, including companies with strong fundamentals and robust EPS growth forecasts. I identified three Strong Buy stocks beaten down in the broader selloff that are showcasing high EPS forward growth rates and bullish earnings revisions from Wall Street analysts.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: Attractive Business, But High Oil Prices Could Be A Problem
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) appears to be an appealing investment candidate for income focussed investors at first sight. The company's well-diversified tenant portfolio, its steadily growing AFFO, the growing cannabis market in the United States, the firm's expansion into the life science market, as well as its valuation, all indicate that IIPR could be and attractive investment candidate right now. But there is one significant risk that we cannot forget, and that high energy need of the indoor cannabis growers, with energy costs making up almost 50% of their operating expenses. If energy prices were to remain high, tenants could face significant challenges, potentially creating headwinds for IIPR as well.

In my article today, I would like to give you a balanced view of IIPR, highlighting both the pros and cons of investing now.

Pros

Diversified tenant portfolio

IIPR has a diversified tenant portfolio, with a total of 38 tenants. None of these tenants account for more than 11% of the company's ABR. In my view this makes IIPR appealing. In the past year, we have seen that the firm has struggled due to the default of some key tenants, e.g. PharmaCann. This put significant downward pressure on the stock price. Having a more diversified revenue stream, and being less reliant on one single tenant definitely makes IIPR business model more robust. Further, the expansion into the life sciences space leads to an even higher degree of diversification.

Return to shareholders

IIPR has been committed to return value to its shareholders over the past years in the form of dividends, and now they also just announced a share buyback program. For income focussed investors, the current annual dividend yield of 14.2% and the $100 million share repurchase program could both be attractive. As a REIT, IIPR is obliged to pay out a certain portion of its profits to the shareholders, and for this reason with forecasted AFFO growth, IIPR will be also obliged to grow its dividends.

Growing cannabis market

While dividends and share buybacks are good, they need to be financed somehow. The continuously growing cannabis market in the U.S. is providing the tailwinds. The industry revenue is expected to increase by as much as 7% CAGR until 2029, with estimated legal sales reaching $44 billion in legal sales. This is supported by the growing legalization in the United States and also by the fact that roughly 800 banks and financial institutions provide services to marijuana-related businesses. Looking forward, I believe that the legalization is going to further spread, increasing the addressable market, benefitting not only the growers, but also IIPR as the provider of facilities.

Growth opportunity

When it comes to growth, it is not only supported by the growing cannabis industry, but also by the expansion into the life sciences market. The life sciences market is also expected to grow at a rate of 7% until 2029, as shown by the firm's expected R&D expenditures in the space. There is also a strong pipeline of potential new approvals from these firms, which could not only benefit the firms developing them, but also IIPR.

Valuation

To value IIPR's stock I will be using a dividend discount model today, as IIPR proved to be a reliable dividend payer that remained committed to return value to its shareholders over the past 8 years.

When it comes to dividend discount models, there are two key input parameters: required rate of return and dividend growth.

For the required rate of return, I generally use the company's cost of equity, which is currently estimated to be 7.34% for IIPR.

For the dividend growth rate in the future, I generally like to use longer-term historic averages in order to stay realistic, However, it may be a too aggressive assumption in IIPR's case. The firm's 5Y historic dividend growth rate CAGR was 11.2%, which would be a very high number to use in perpetuity. The 3Y CAGR dividend growth rate is only 2.29%, which is more realistic, but I wanted to goo even more conservative this time. For the first case, I assumed 0% dividend growth in perpetuity, and that resulted in a fair value of $111 per share, roughly double the current share price.

This made me wonder, what the stock might be priced for currently. To end up with the current share price of $55, I needed to assume a 50% dividend cut next year, and 0% in perpetuity. I believe this is a highly unlikely scenario.

For these reasons, I believe that IIPR at the current price could provide significant upside potential.

Cons

In the first section of this writing, I put IIPR in a very good light. But it is not the whole story. There are significant risks that we need to consider.

Oil price

Cannabis growers are sensitive to the oil price fluctuations, as energy costs could represent as much as 50% of the indoor growers' operating costs. With the oil prices skyrocketing in the recent weeks due to the ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, growers might be hit hard, especially if the prices were to stay elevated for longer.

The high oil prices have far reaching consequences. Elevated energy prices normally drive inflation higher, which could mean that the Fed would keep interest rates higher, for longer. Higher interest rates mean higher cost of debt. For firm's relying on debt financing, this could be a significant headwind.

Regulatory threats

The cannabis business is highly reliant on the regulation and where the regulation is expected to head. If trends were to change, and further legalization would be delayed or hindered, that could have a material negative impact on IIPR's tenants, and therefore on the security of IIPR revenue stream.

Conclusions

Innovative Industrial Properties appear attractive from many different angles. The firm has a diversified tenant base, they pay attractive dividends to the shareholders, they are expanding into the life sciences market, and their share also appear to be attractively value right now.

There are significant risks that need to be taken into account, however. One of them is the cannabis growers' high dependance on energy. Roughly 50% of the indoor growers' operating expenses are the energy cost. If oil prices were to remain elevated for a longer period, this could hurt IIPR's tenants, leading to potential defaults on the leases. Regulatory threats related to legalization also need to be closely followed.

All in all, however, I believe that IIPR is to cheap to ignore at the current price levels, and for this reason I rate it as a buy.

data.title: Ketchup Or Catch-Down? Kraft Heinz Is Racing Against Structural Decline
data.description:
The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) has had a terrible year being down 24% (including dividends) while the broader consumer staples industry was mildly up at 6.5%. Despite a large drawdown for a defensive company, we remain very cautious regarding its future prospects. We believe the company and the packaged foods industry in general will continue to face significant headwinds.

Structural decline of package foods industry

Once prized for their defensiveness and steady dividend growth, packaged food stocks have gradually lost their luster since the "Nifty Fifty" consumer staples era. Today, we believe the underperformance of KHC and packaged food broadly boils down to several structural and cyclical forces.

Private label "supercycle": U.S. private label sales reached $283B in 2025, outpacing national brand's 1.2% Y/Y growth with a 3.3% Y/Y figure, according to data compiled by the Private Label Manufacturers Association. Households that earn over $100K are leaning increasingly towards store brands, and 71% of U.S. shoppers now believe private label quality is at least as good as national brands. Retailers like Costco (COST) and Walmart (WMT) are expanding their private label strategies with success.

KHC faced volume erosion: Across nearly every category, which includes cold cuts, condiments, frozen meals, and coffee, KHC saw declines in organic net sales of -2.5%/-4.2% Y/Y in 3Q25/4Q25, while reducing its full-year outlook many times. It has guided organic net sales to decline -1.5% to -3.5% Y/Y on $24.9B in FY25 sales, and adjusted EPS to drift lower to $1.98 to $2.10, compared to $2.60 reported in FY25.

Brand value impairment: The company took a $9.3B in non-cash impairment charge in late 2025 due to falling brand equity. This was based on the difference between current fair value assessment and the value estimated for the original merger led by 3G Capital/Berkshire in 2015, which promised to unlock aggressive cost-cutting value.

Failed M&A logic: The premise on which the merger rests was that staples brands needed zero marketing/reinvestment spend. This proved to be a disastrous failure because the market shares of these unattended brands eroded severely. The plan in 2025 to split the company into a global tastes company and a North American grocery company was shelved due to weak earnings. Instead, another $600M will be redirected to the reinvestment of the whole company.

Changing health trends: U.S. consumers are structurally reducing caloric consumption, via weight-loss drugs and dietary intake changes. Consumers are more aware of the health risks associated with processed food and are increasingly opting for fresh foods.

Outlook and more uncertainty

The company has guided FY2026 to be a "reset" year with a $600M in reinvestment outlay to hopefully recapture its lost market share. FY26E EPS of $1.98–$2.10 is guided to be a -19% to -24% Y/Y decline while net organic sales will only drop between -1.5% and -3.5% Y/Y.

We don't think it is possible to turn around its U.S operations due to the major shifts in health trends we mentioned earlier. Absent new and innovative food products, the U.S. business will likely falter from a slow bleed of low to mid single digit Y/Y sales declines.

Additionally, there is still the question of tariffs. Despite IEEPA tariffs being outlawed, the administration has begun exploring implementing tariffs under other sections. The company embedded ~$150M-$200M tariff headwind into its FY26E guidance, but the Section 122 escalation in February 2026 was not fully contemplated.

Are there any bright spots?

Emerging markets are still a growth driver for KHC. These regions grew net organic sales at 4.6% Y/Y but carry much lower adjusted operating margins at 12.1% vs 23.6% for North America, in FY25. But only at 11.3% geographic revenue mix, emerging markets' strength is no where near enough to offset developed market weakness.

The dividend is safe, still. KHC pays $0.40/quarter per share, unchanged since the 36% cut in 2019. At current prices, its dividend yield is ~7%. KHC generated $3.66B in FCF in FY25, and with a dividend payout of $1.9B, its FCF payout ratio is 51.8%, better than the 61.1% in FY24.

We map out how several scenarios that may hit FCF in FY26E:

Scenario FCF FCF Payout Cushion
Base (consensus) $2.63B 72.1% +$736M
Bull (+$200M FCF upside) $2.83B 67.0% +$936M
Mild Stress (-$200M) $2.43B 78.0% +$536M
Bear (-$400M) $2.23B 85.0% +$336M
Stress (-$600M) $2.03B 93.3% +$136M

Source: Himalayas Research estimates

It does appear FCF coverage is robust enough, so dividend is not a real risk yet.

Condiments improving: the CEO has cited on the Q4 call that Heinz and Philadelphia Cream Cheese have had stronger momentum in the 13-weeks leading up to the call. If the company eventually ends up splitting, the carve-out that operates the Taste Elevation segment may be the better pick.

Valuation and verdict

KHC trades at cheap valuations compared to the broad market and its historical averages. Its forward P/E ratio is 10.8X, versus a five-year mean of 12.6X. Its forward EV/EBITDA is 8.8X, compared with a five-year mean of 10.1X.

But against a tough environment, we cannot recommend buying KHC stock which houses two iconic, century-old companies. While we can understand the allure of buying a downtrodden household name that bears about 7% dividend yield, the structural threat is too great to ignore.

data.title: Ichor: Semiconductor Turnaround Continues With Significant Upside
data.description:
Ichor Holdings (ICHR) has been a historically strong performer in the semiconductor wafer fabrication market. Ichor is a small cap player in the gas panel system business currently at $1.66 Billion market cap, an essential partner for modern Fabs with increasing complexity. However, in the previous semiconductor cycle the company lagged. Things may be beginning to change, with industry stalwart ASML calling out improvements in 2026 expectations. Market participants are beginning to expect a broadening of semiconductor demand into 2026 which should benefit Ichor as their biggest customers are the two giants in the space - Lam Research (LRCX) and Applied Materials (AMAT).

The company has lagged its larger peers but this could be due for a snapback in the coming year if the semiconductor bull continues. I last wrote about Ichor way back in 2017 seen here when it was on a run of significant acquisitions. This has given ICHR a strong position in the creation of many components but ultimately the company hasn't been able to capitalize to grow market share. The stock has only increased 70% since, trailing the market significantly as contract manufacturers have struggled to increase importance. Now things may be beginning to change as the industry is in need of supply and capacity ICHR can provide. Signs are beginning to form that Lam Research and Applied Materials have drawn down inventory. That could prove bullish for the companies prospects as revenue flows down the supply chain to the contract manufacturers like ICHR. The company has been staking concrete steps to improve margins after a long period of underperformance, giving it a potential valuation catchup throughout 2026. Recent Q4 results have shined a light on the traction management has gained of late, with the Iranian war pullback giving an excellent entry for new buyers in the shares.

Solid Q4 a sign of things to come

Ichor has been a weak performer in the space over the past 10 years, with a solid niche in gas panels hampered by poor execution. The company has struggled to improve its market share and has a multitude of suppliers that are preventing it from achieving solid gross margins and operating profit. However, the team at ICHR is starting to fix these issues by increasing the amount of ICHR content in its products. As the company adds its own valves, filters, fittings and flow controllers it will improve its margin profile by keeping that profit in house. This should lead to better margins over time, with this being a multi-year story of turnaround. It's main customers are finally back to strong forecasts, which bodes well for ICHR as it works to improve its own standing while supplying significantly more product in 2026. Improvement in gross margin from 11% to 15% by the end of this year should set the company up for solid earnings in 2027 with continued strength in WFE. AI applications are improving the outlook as gate-all-around and advanced packaging are supporting strong growth for the next 2 years or more. This is increasing etch and deposition intensity leading to a strong upcycle for equipment players like Ichor. Visibility our more than 1 year now is strong which will help support multiples this cycle at a higher level than some in recent history. Ichor had $223.6 million of revenue in the December quarter, showing a strong guide with $250 million at the midpoint for the current quarter. Indications from ICHR as well as industry peers point to lowered inventory levels at their major customers, necessitating increasing purchases in calendar 2026. The company even called out revenue growth in every quarter in 2026 in the Q1 conference call, a nice data point with 2H revenue then expected to be above $250 million per quarter. At only 2.2% operating margin for the year 2025, ICHR has a lot of upside to get back to former operating margin highs of 8%+ in 2018. This is a base case bull scenario, with potential upside from acquisitions and improvements in margin or wafer fabrication growth. The company did call out that they aren't modeling for 18-20% gross margin yet, but they did say significant operating margin leverage is coming as they scale in the next 6-7 quarters.

The balance sheet is solid with $98.3 million in cash on hand, helping fund share repurchases, capacity additions and tuck in acquisitions. ICHR does hold $123m in debt but with positive $6m in cash flow in Q4 improving through 2026 it shouldn't hold the business back. The market is hoping ICHR focuses on its core business right now and it seems any acquisitions may be pushed out until 2027 as a result. They are adding significant capacity for machining this year in Mexico as well as ICHR's largest manufacturer center to date in Malaysia. These low cost jurisdictions should help bring costs down over the long term as well once they get ramped into 2027. Below you can see the improvement is partially due to leverage we are already seeing in revenue. Cost reductions and increasing content of parts made in-house are also significant contributors. Some of these improvements will continue into 2027 as well helping the company get back to a cash generative position.

The technical picture has moderated after the Iran conflict began as the high growth stocks took a significant hit amid the selling. However, this has position ICHR now in a good spot for new money as the medium and long term trends are both strong. The recent move upwards as the company started getting 2026 visibly was quite violent with some sellers coming in after the Iran conflict started. As long as the shares are above the 50 day MA the shares are in a strong upward trend and buying on dips is recommended. Currently the shares are down $8 from highs, allowing for a good entry into shares that have a long period of stagnation since its strong run in 2021. Institutional investors continue to add with 40 institutions adding in 4Q against 31 reducing positions as the investment cycle in semiconductors picks up pace.

Buy Rated - Significant runway for improvement

While the semiconductor cycle has been underway for some time among the favored players, it has only just begun for the contract manufacturers. They have struggled in recent years, but this stronger cycle is going to renew their importance to the overall supply chain. Also, Ichor is making significant improvements fundamentally to their business model after struggling for some time. Some of the good news is priced in now after the past 3 months but the shares still have significant upside as 2027 estimates start to form. Those looking for a small cap option in the semiconductor space would do well to purchase ICHR shares below the recent highs of $55.

data.title: Canadian Solar: Hammered After Earnings, Still A Hold
data.description:
Thesis

Canadian Solar Inc. (CSIQ) is a global company that makes solar products and develops energy storage and solar projects. The last time I reviewed the stock, I rated it as a Hold because profit margins were being squeezed, the company was losing cash, and there were still too many risks in how well they could execute their plans to justify buying into the story.

Since then, CSIQ stock’s been down hard, and today’s not making it better. Be that as it may, after reviewing today’s latest results, I’m maintaining my neutral view. The core challenges remain unresolved, and the path to a sustained recovery is uncertain, but the valuation already reflects much of the downside.

My View on Canadian Solar’s Q4 and FY2025 Results

  • Q4 2025 module shipments: 4.3 GW
  • FY2025 module shipments: 24.3 GW
  • FY2025 energy storage shipments: 7.8 GWh.

The fourth quarter ended what had already been a tough 2025 for Canadian Solar. During the year, the company chose not to focus on selling as many solar modules as possible in a weak global market. Instead, it focused on protecting its profit margins and expanding into other areas to reduce risk. The management team clearly said they focused more on higher-value areas, like energy storage, instead of just increasing the amount of products they shipped. It’s another indication the industry hasn’t fixed its basic problems: too much supply, sliding prices, and a demand recovery that’s been slow and unreliable.

  • Q4 2025 revenue: $1.217B (-20.0% Y/Y) misses by $150M.

With solar module deliveries down both sequentially and year-over-year, total revenue took the predictable hit. At the same time, the energy storage business achieved record shipments for the full year, even though deliveries in the fourth quarter were slightly lower due to construction delays at customer sites.

  • Q4 2025 gross margin: 10.2%
  • Q3 2025 gross margin: 17.2%
  • Q4 2024 gross margin: 14.3%
  • Q4 2025 gross profit: $124M.

Profit margins dropped this quarter. This was mainly due to lower storage volumes, smaller contributions from module sales in North America, delays in selling some projects, and losses in value (impairments) on certain project assets. The drop compared to the previous period was especially large because of those losses in asset value. Earnings fell from both module and project sales compared to last year. And while module prices were higher, they didn’t fully close the gap.

  • Q4 2025 operating expenses: $188M
  • Q4 2025 net income: -$86M.

Meanwhile, operating expenses came down compared to earlier periods, mainly because logistics costs were lower. Business slowed, but the company made sure costs didn’t get the same idea. Even so, it reported a net loss due to lower gross profit, ongoing interest costs from having a high level of debt, and other factors such as changes in working capital.

  • Q4 2025 operating cash flow: -$65M.

Cash flow from operations went negative this quarter, mainly because they put more money into project assets as they kept building out the pipeline. That said, inventory came down a bit, which helped offset some of the drag.

  • FY2025 capex: $962M
  • Cash (end 2025): $1.37B.

And capex stayed high all year since they kept pushing hard on the U.S. manufacturing build-out, although it came in a bit under the original plan. Despite all that, the company has a strong cash balance. This is partly because it issued a convertible bond toward the end of the year, which brought in new funds to help accelerate its domestic production efforts.

  • Total debt: $6.5B.

Debt went up as a result of loans taken out to fund new construction projects. Most of these are non-recourse and tied to individual projects, meaning the company itself isn’t directly responsible if something goes wrong. Nevertheless, because this type of business is capital-intensive, the debt’s still sitting there, and it’s not going to watch itself.

In response to these pressures, management's putting more focus on bringing operations back to the United States. They are doing this by taking direct control of their U.S. business through the CS PowerTech platform, increasing production at the Texas module factory, and moving forward with the Indiana cell facility. At the Indiana site, phase one testing is about to begin. Phase two is still on track.

Companies are leaning more into producing content locally, mostly because government policy doesn’t sit still for long. And as those rules change, it just makes more sense to stay closer to home. Recent laws, such as the OBBBA, have introduced stricter rules about using domestically sourced materials. They've also shortened the time companies have to qualify for certain incentives and added new restrictions on involvement from foreign entities. Ongoing trade investigations and early tariffs, like the high countervailing duties on imports from India, inflates costs and ties supply chains in knots. This is why it makes more sense for companies to build and produce goods locally.

There are still risks in putting this into action. Increasing new cell production is technically difficult and not the easiest thing to deal with. At the same time, in early 2026 there'll likely be shortages of approved cells that aren't made by foreign entities, which could slow down U.S. module shipments and reduce profits.

  • Q1 2026 revenue guidance: $0.9B–$1.1B
  • Q1 2026 gross margin guidance: 13%–15%
  • Q1 2026 module shipments: 2.2–2.4 GW
  • Q1 2026 storage shipments: 1.7–1.9 GWh.

Looking ahead, management says that 2026 will be a transition year. The company expects to deal with changing and unpredictable costs for materials, uncertain government policies, and short-term supply shortages in the United States. These issues should improve later in the year, as increased domestic cell production in the second half of 2026 helps reduce the pressure. Nevertheless, they expect lower demand in the first quarter, mainly because of normal seasonal slowdowns. Most of the shipments during this time will go toward catching up on storage orders that were delayed earlier.

Deep Discount Meets Slowing Recovery

After today’s earnings news, CSIQ's now trading at about $13.50 per share, and this price (on yesterday's data) looks extremely cheap to me. Even before the stock dropped today, it was already priced at a level that made it seem like a real bargain, so assuming this massive selloff holds, these metrics, once adjusted for the closing price, technically should only improve.

Even at $18.52 the shares already looked like a deep bargain: 0.21x trailing sales, 0.44x book, EV at roughly 1.3x sales. Drop the price to $13.50 and those same metrics compress further to about 0.15x sales and 0.32x book while the FAST Graphs model pushes the implied annual return even higher.

Its models suggested a possible 145% yearly return once the company’s earnings return to normal and the expected long-term EPS growth rate of 73% starts to take effect.

There's a problem. Earnings landed only hours ago. That triple-digit implied return was always an aggressive mathematical output. After today’s report it looks way more speculative. In today's transcript, management never endorses or even remotely hints at that kind of compounding.

In the short term, they expect some clear challenges. There isn’t enough supply of solar cells that meet current U.S. rules (non-PFE), rising material costs are cutting into profits, and shipments of solar modules in the U.S. are expected to be a bit lower in the first half of 2026 compared to 2025. They expect things to improve only after their own solar cell production lines in Indiana start running more fully in Q2/Q3. It suggests a slow, uneven recovery. No real case for strong double-digit earnings growth anytime soon.

The only areas of growth they point to are their record $3.6 billion energy storage backlog and the expansion of their manufacturing operations in the United States.

These are real positives, yes. But they also make it clear there are risks, like actually delivering on those projects, managing cash flow, and dealing with uncertain government policies.

Final Takeaway

The contrarian in me almost nudged me to buy here. But all things considered, I'm keeping my Hold rating. The potential upside does look appealing for patient investors if the shift to U.S. manufacturing works out and the energy storage business grows. Even so, there're still significant risks here, including high cash usage, and uncertainty around government policies. But if you strongly believe in bringing solar manufacturing back to the U.S. and are comfortable taking on higher risk, it may make sense to buy small amounts selectively. Should this selloff get heavier, I'll start dipping my toes in the water.

data.title: VEA: Lower Correlations And Cheaper Valuations Make The Case
data.description:
Intro

Developed markets are a sensible way to invest for many reasons. The investor may want exposure to different foreign currencies or they may want to diversify away from their own domestic market due to valuations or geopolitical risks. Or perhaps they want to include an equity that offers lower correlation benefits to improve the risk-adjusted return of a total portfolio, like myself.

In this article, I'm going to cover the correlation benefits by including a developed market fund in a globally diversified portfolio. l will then cover why I prefer VEA to target this asset class. And lastly, I will explain why I believe it's a great time to enter a position or rebalance into you haven't already.

Why Choose Developed Markets?

Since my last coverage of Vanguard Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA), it has performed pretty well against the market. Up roughly 22% versus the S& 500 at 12.90% on a total return basis. Although this is a short window, its nice to see some of the developed market premium show itself even in the face of some headwinds in the volatile oil market and tariffs.

One of the reasons it's held strong is because VEA is very diversified among several different industries and countries. For example, energy only makes up 4-5% so while the impact was felt, it still has other holdings to fall back on. It's also not concentrated like the S&P 500. The top 10 holdings for VEA only make up around 11% of portfolio weight where the market is concentrated nearly 35% due to the magnificent 7. This could mean there is less risk tied to VEA currently, but may also explain a main benefit being correlation , or lack thereof, to the market.

One prime reason someone would want to include developed markets in their portfolio is because this distinct asset class tends to provide some anti-correlation to a domestic fund such as US Large cap. This means it could exhibit periods of outperformance which help produce better risk-adjusted returns. On average, developed market show roughly 0.85 correlation to US Large cap which means it moves in lockstep around 85% with the other 15% moving in different directions. Recently however, developed markets show about 60% correlation. This is because developed markets, as measured by VEA returns are up 3.5% while US Large Cap is down -1.5% YTD.

Furthermore, rolling return spreads show developed markets may be attractive currently. Historically, whenever they underperform against the market, the relative expected returns are more attractive signaling a better time to buy to rebalance a greater weighting towards developed markets.

The Seeking Alpha quant system is also signaling a good time to buy. Reducing correlations, attractive return spreads all point me to favoring developed markets currently.

On top of all this, VEA total valuation is at 17-18 times earnings. Compare this to a Shiller PE in the 40s for the total market and you can see why there are a lot of strong reasons why I believe developed markets are attractive.

Why is VEA a Good Fund to Target Developed Markets?

So this raises the question - if developed markets provide excellent correlation benefits, and are at a seemingly attractive relative valuation, why do I consider VEA to be an excellent fund to access this asset class?

Well in general, most developed funds will target the asset class correctly as measured by correlations to the benchmark - which means the next two main factors of fund selection are the assets under management "AUM" or liquidity, and the fund cost.

VEA has an expense ratio of 0.03% which is the lowest or tied to the lowest for peers via Seeking Alpha. On top of this, $222B in AUM outpaces all peers by a landslide with the rest of the funds hovering between $27B and $71B. In other words, VEA is the most liquid and supported while also being the cheapest.

From a total return basis, VEA posts a 52.31% over the last five years coming in second behind the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF) at 53.39%. However from a risk standpoint, VEA has the second largest holdings and the least amount of assets in the top 10.

This means VEA will have more attractive risk-adjusted metrics such as sharpe ratio if these trends continue.

Risks

The main risk here is that the recent drop in correlation doesn’t stick. If developed markets move back into tighter lockstep with U.S. large caps, which has historically been the norm, then a drawdown in U.S. equities would likely pull VEA down with it. I think some reasons why this may not fully revert are if valuation spreads remain wide, with international markets trading at a meaningful discount, which can drive differentiated returns over time. On top of that, sector composition is very different. Developed markets have less exposure to mega-cap tech and more to financials and industrials, so they’re not driven by the same earnings engines. Currency movements can also act as a wedge, especially in periods where the U.S. dollar weakens. So while correlations can and do shift, the current backdrop suggests there are still enough structural differences in play to support some level of diversification going forward.

Why is it a Good Buy Today?

At the end of the day, developed markets are starting to do what you actually want them to do which is to diversify. Correlations have come down, performance has started to pick up, and valuations are meaningfully more attractive than what you’re getting in the U.S. right now. That’s usually the setup you want to see. When an asset class has lagged, gotten cheaper, and sentiment isn’t crowded, forward returns tend to look better and we’re starting to see that play out. VEA is just a really clean way to access it. It’s cheap, extremely liquid, and broadly diversified without taking on unnecessary concentration risk. If you’ve let U.S. equities run and haven’t rebalanced in a while, this is one of those spots where it probably makes sense to start leaning back into developed markets.

As it stands today, I subscribe to a 10% weighting in a globally diversified portfolio to VEA.

data.title: Bank of Canada Admits It Can't 'Save' Bubbles
data.description:
The Bank of Canada press conference yesterday finally admitted facts about Canada’s housing market: “The housing market is looking weaker, and weaker than we had incorporated into our January outlook. So, that’s something we will be looking at when we prepare the April outlook.” Canadian home prices “need to come down” and “housing feeds into our forecast and deliberations on monetary policy.”

In other words, interventionist central banks can help balloon debt and asset bubbles, but they can’t stop them from bursting. Here’s the unvarnished video clip.

As the materials’ bubble continues to implode, silver (-44%) since the end of January and gold (-18%), the TSX has fallen just over 6% since March 2, 2026 and is now flat year to date. Parabolic oil prices are bad for everything.

The masses are learning once again that all the marketing hype about “defensive” sectors is the same high risk in different shiny wrappers. Here’s David Rosenberg this morning:

There are few places to hide. Not in stocks - every sector closed in the red. Not in Energy stocks, even with the spike in crude prices. Not in the most defensive sectors, like Heath Care and Consumer Staples, which actually underperformed the market. Not in gold, silver, Bitcoin, or industrial metals, and that includes once-mighty copper. Even the S&P 500 Defense subsector fell by -0.3% yesterday and has been clocked for losses in seven of the past eight sessions. What does the Iran war have to do with U.S. Health Care Service stocks? Yet this group sagged by more than -1.0% for the second day in a row and is off by nearly -10% in less than two weeks.

For those who love to talk about the resilience of the overall equity market, dig a little deeper and you will see that the regional banks - big lenders for private debt and equity funds - are back in a bear market (down -20% from the early February highs). Consumer Finance stocks are off by -26% (back to where they were last June) and yet nobody from the media asked Jay Powell at yesterday’s press conference how that fits into the “economy is solid” narrative. All the more with General Mills (GIS) and Macy’s (M) having just issued some soggy guidance over the consumer outlook.

On a similar note, see Private Credit’s Investor Exodus Spreads to Consumer Loans:

A fund holding consumer and small-business loans made by companies including Affirm (AFRM) and Block (XYZ) is the latest corner of the private-credit market to come under stress.

Stone Ridge Asset Management told clients in the fund last week that recent redemption requests were so high that it would honor only 11% of the amount investors wanted back, according to an investor update viewed by The Wall Street Journal.

That suggests that investors’ concerns about private credit are broadening. Unlike other private-credit funds that experienced a flight of investors in recent weeks, Stone Ridge’s fund didn’t hold loans to software makers or other corporate sectors that investors fear will be displaced by advances in artificial intelligence.

Cash and short- and medium-term treasuries remain the most attractive options until other asset prices return to investment-grade levels down from the latest speculative mania.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
